agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress channels (mobile, partner, internal), centralizing cross-cutting concerns while maintaining strict traffic isolation. This eliminates the current anti-pattern of distributed auth implementations and creates a single enforcement point for security, rate limiting, and observability.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway (Edge)","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic. Terminates TLS, performs authentication, enforces rate limits, routes requests to upstream services. Runs as a cluster of stateless instances behind a load balancer.","boundaries":"Handles ONLY cross-cutting concerns: auth, rate limiting, request validation, routing, logging. Contains zero business logic. Never transforms domain payloads beyond header enrichment."},{"name":"Mobile Ingress Channel","responsibility":"Dedicated listener/route group for mobile app traffic. Enforces JWT/OAuth2 bearer token authentication and 100 req/min per-user rate limiting. Applies mobile-specific request size limits and timeout policies.","boundaries":"Logically isolated route configuration within the gateway. Shares gateway infrastructure but has independent rate limit counters and auth pipeline."},{"name":"Partner Ingress Channel","responsibility":"Dedicated listener/route group for B2B partner traffic. Enforces API key authentication, 1,000 req/min per-partner rate limiting, IP allowlisting (optional), and request signing verification.","boundaries":"Isolated from mobile traffic. Partner identity resolved from API key, enriched into headers for downstream services."},{"name":"Internal Ingress Channel","responsibility":"Dedicated listener/route group bound to private network interface only. No authentication required. Enforces network-level isolation (private subnet, security groups). Applies basic rate limiting as a safety valve (10,000 req/min per service).","boundaries":"MUST NOT be reachable from public internet. Bound to internal NLB or private DNS only. Separate from external gateway instances if infrastructure supports it."},{"name":"Rate Limit Store","responsibility":"Centralized sliding-window rate limit state. Redis cluster storing counters keyed by consumer identity (user ID, partner API key, service name). Shared across all gateway instances for consistent enforcement.","boundaries":"Accessed only by the gateway. Not exposed to upstream services. Failure mode: fail-open with local fallback counters and alerting."},{"name":"Auth Provider","responsibility":"Issues and validates tokens. For mobile: OAuth2/OIDC provider issuing JWTs. For partners: API key registry with key metadata (partner ID, tier, allowed scopes). Gateway delegates validation here.","boundaries":"Separate service from the gateway. Gateway caches validation results (JWT signature verification is local; API key lookups cached with short TTL)."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Manages webhook subscriptions for partners. Receives domain events from internal services, transforms them to partner-facing payloads, delivers with retry logic (exponential backoff, dead letter queue).","boundaries":"Sits behind the gateway for subscription management APIs. Outbound delivery bypasses the gateway (direct egress). Owns its own delivery state and retry persistence."},{"name":"Upstream Services","responsibility":"Business logic services (order management, tracking, fleet, etc.). After gateway migration, these services shed ALL auth and rate limiting code. They trust the gateway's enriched headers (X-Consumer-Type, X-Consumer-ID, X-Partner-ID).","boundaries":"Receive only pre-authenticated, rate-limited traffic. Must validate X-Consumer-Type header to enforce authorization (what a consumer CAN do), but authentication (who they are) is settled."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"API Gateway (Mobile Ingress)","contract":"HTTPS with Bearer JWT in Authorization header. Gateway validates JWT signature and expiry, extracts user ID, injects X-Consumer-Type: mobile, X-User-ID: <id>","coupling_type":"loose — standard OAuth2 bearer flow"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"API Gateway (Partner Ingress)","contract":"HTTPS with X-API-Key header. Gateway resolves partner identity, validates key status and scopes, injects X-Consumer-Type: partner, X-Partner-ID: <id>, X-Partner-Tier: <tier>","coupling_type":"loose — API key is opaque identifier"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"API Gateway (Internal Ingress)","contract":"HTTP over private network. No auth headers required. Gateway injects X-Consumer-Type: internal, X-Service-Name: <name> based on mTLS client cert or service mesh identity.","coupling_type":"platform — relies on network isolation"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Upstream Services","contract":"HTTP with enriched headers. Gateway forwards original request body untouched. Adds X-Consumer-Type, X-Consumer-ID, X-Request-ID, X-Rate-Limit-Remaining headers.","coupling_type":"loose — header contract, no payload transformation"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Rate Limit Store (Redis)","contract":"INCR with TTL on composite key (consumer_type:consumer_id:window). Returns current count. Gateway compares against tier-specific threshold.","coupling_type":"operational — gateway depends on Redis availability"},{"from":"Upstream Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to message broker (e.g., SQS/SNS/Kafka). Webhook dispatcher subscribes and delivers to registered partner endpoints.","coupling_type":"loose — async event-driven, no direct coupling"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"Webhook Dispatcher (via Gateway)","contract":"REST API for managing webhook subscriptions (CRUD). Authenticated through Partner Ingress channel.","coupling_type":"loose — standard REST"}],"data_flow":"External request → Load Balancer → API Gateway (TLS termination) → Channel identification (by hostname, path prefix, or listener port) → Authentication pipeline (JWT validation OR API key lookup OR network identity) → Rate limit check against Redis (sliding window) → Route resolution → Header enrichment → Forward to upstream service → Response flows back through gateway (adds rate limit headers, strips internal headers, logs metrics)","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"API Gateway / Backend for Frontend (BFF-lite)","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns that were inconsistently implemented across services. Three ingress channels act as lightweight BFFs without duplicating gateway infrastructure."},{"pattern":"Sliding Window Rate Limiting","rationale":"More accurate than fixed-window counters (avoids burst-at-boundary problem). Redis MULTI/EXEC with sorted sets gives precise sliding window semantics."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Services currently handle their own auth. Migrate one service at a time behind the gateway rather than big-bang cutover. Gateway can initially pass-through auth headers while services still validate, then services shed auth code once gateway is proven."},{"pattern":"Header Enrichment / Claims Propagation","rationale":"Gateway resolves consumer identity once and propagates it via headers. Upstream services never re-authenticate — they authorize based on enriched identity headers."},{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker on Rate Limit Store","rationale":"If Redis is unavailable, gateway falls back to local in-memory approximate counters rather than either blocking all traffic or allowing unlimited traffic."},{"pattern":"Async Event-Driven Webhooks","rationale":"Decouples webhook delivery from request processing. Partners get reliable delivery via retry queues without upstream services needing to know about webhooks."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"API Access Control","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["ConsumerIdentity (root: consumer_id, type, status, rate_limit_tier)","APIKey (root: key_hash, partner_id, scopes, expiry, status)","RateLimitPolicy (root: consumer_type, requests_per_window, window_size)"]},{"name":"Webhook Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["WebhookSubscription (root: subscription_id, partner_id, event_types, target_url, secret, status)","DeliveryAttempt (root: attempt_id, subscription_id, payload, status, retry_count)"]},{"name":"Logistics Core","classification":"core","aggregates":["(existing domain aggregates — orders, shipments, tracking, fleet — untouched by gateway design)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Logistics Core","downstream":"Webhook Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Core publishes domain events, Webhook Management subscribes and transforms for partner delivery. Core does not know about webhooks."},{"upstream":"API Access Control","downstream":"API Gateway","relationship":"Open Host Service — Gateway consumes access control policies and consumer registry via well-defined internal API. Gateway is conformist to the access control model."},{"upstream":"API Access Control","downstream":"Webhook Management","relationship":"Shared Kernel — both contexts share the concept of Partner identity (partner_id, tier). Minimal shared kernel limited to partner identifier."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single gateway cluster with logical channel separation vs. physically separate gateways per consumer type","gains":"Operational simplicity — one deployment, one config system, shared observability. Lower infrastructure cost. Easier to maintain consistent behavior.","sacrifices":"Blast radius is larger — a gateway bug affects all consumer types. Resource contention possible under extreme load from one channel.","alternatives_considered":["Separate gateway per consumer type: stronger isolation but 3x operational overhead and config drift risk","Service mesh only (no gateway): works for internal but doesn't solve external auth centralization"]},{"decision":"Redis for rate limiting vs. local in-memory counters","gains":"Accurate global rate limiting across all gateway instances. Consistent enforcement regardless of which instance handles the request.","sacrifices":"Adds Redis as a runtime dependency. Network latency on every request (mitigated by sub-millisecond Redis response times). Requires Redis HA setup.","alternatives_considered":["Local counters only: no dependency but inaccurate with multiple gateway instances (each instance tracks independently)","Token bucket in database: too slow for request-path enforcement"]},{"decision":"Strangler fig migration vs. big-bang cutover","gains":"Low risk — migrate one service at a time, validate gateway behavior incrementally. Can roll back individual services without affecting others.","sacrifices":"Transition period where some services are behind gateway and some aren't. Temporary complexity of dual auth paths.","alternatives_considered":["Big-bang cutover: cleaner end-state faster but extremely high risk for a running production system","Shadow mode only: adds complexity of running gateway in parallel without enforcing — delays value delivery"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Rate limit tiers","evolution_path":"Rate limits are configuration-driven per consumer type and tier. Adding a new tier (e.g., premium partners at 5,000 req/min) requires only a config change, no code deployment."},{"area":"Authentication methods","evolution_path":"Auth pipeline is pluggable per channel. Adding mTLS for partners or passkey support for mobile requires adding an auth plugin to that channel's pipeline without affecting others."},{"area":"New consumer types","evolution_path":"Adding a fourth ingress channel (e.g., public developer API) requires a new route group and auth/rate-limit config. No architectural changes needed."},{"area":"Gateway technology choice","evolution_path":"Architecture is implementation-agnostic. Can start with Kong/AWS API Gateway/Envoy and migrate later. The enriched-header contract between gateway and services is the stable interface."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Header enrichment contract","reason":"X-Consumer-Type, X-Consumer-ID, X-Request-ID headers are the interface between gateway and all upstream services. Changing these header names or semantics requires coordinated changes across every service. Intentionally rigid — this is the foundational trust contract."},{"area":"Network isolation for internal channel","reason":"Internal traffic isolation relies on infrastructure-level network segmentation (VPC, security groups, private subnets). This must be non-negotiable and verified by automated infrastructure tests. A misconfiguration here exposes internal APIs to the internet."},{"area":"Rate limit store consistency model","reason":"Sliding window in Redis with sorted sets. Changing the algorithm (e.g., to token bucket) affects all consumers simultaneously. Intentionally rigid to ensure predictable, auditable rate limiting behavior."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Gateway becomes single point of failure for all external traffic","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy gateway as stateless cluster behind load balancer with health checks. Minimum 3 instances across availability zones. Auto-scaling based on request rate. Chaos test gateway instance failures regularly."},{"risk":"Redis rate limit store failure blocks all requests","severity":"high","mitigation":"Circuit breaker pattern: if Redis is unreachable for >100ms, fall back to local approximate counters with degraded accuracy. Alert immediately. Redis deployed as HA cluster with automatic failover."},{"risk":"Internal channel accidentally exposed to public internet","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Infrastructure-as-code with automated compliance checks. Internal channel binds to private network interface only. Periodic penetration testing. Security group rules verified in CI/CD pipeline."},{"risk":"Inconsistent auth removal from upstream services during migration","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Strangler fig with shadow mode: gateway authenticates AND services still validate during transition. Remove service-level auth only after gateway has been proven stable for that service (>2 weeks, monitored). Feature flag per service to toggle auth delegation."},{"risk":"Webhook delivery reliability — partner endpoints may be unreliable","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Exponential backoff retry (1s, 2s, 4s... up to 1 hour). Dead letter queue after max retries. Dashboard for partners to see delivery status and replay failed deliveries. HMAC signature on payloads so partners can verify authenticity."},{"risk":"Gateway latency overhead on every request","severity":"medium","mitigation":"JWT validation is local (no network call — just signature verification against cached public key). API key lookups cached in gateway memory with 60s TTL. Redis rate limit check adds <1ms. Target: <5ms total gateway overhead. Monitor P99 latency."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): Deploy gateway in shadow mode — all traffic passes through but auth decisions are logged, not enforced. Measure latency overhead and validate routing correctness. Phase 2 (Weeks 4-6): Enable auth enforcement on gateway for 1-2 low-risk services. Services still validate auth as backup. Monitor error rates. Phase 3 (Weeks 7-10): Migrate remaining services behind gateway. Remove redundant auth code from services one at a time. Phase 4 (Weeks 11-12): Enable partner webhook management, complete rate limiting rollout, harden monitoring.","files_to_create_or_modify":["gateway/config/routes.yaml — route definitions per channel (mobile, partner, internal)","gateway/config/rate-limits.yaml — rate limit policies per consumer type/tier","gateway/plugins/auth-jwt.lua|js — JWT validation plugin for mobile channel","gateway/plugins/auth-apikey.lua|js — API key validation plugin for partner channel","gateway/plugins/rate-limiter.lua|js — sliding window rate limiter with Redis backend","gateway/plugins/header-enrichment.lua|js — injects X-Consumer-Type/ID/Request-ID headers","services/shared/middleware/trust-gateway-headers.ts — middleware for upstream services to read enriched headers and drop legacy auth code","infra/terraform/api-gateway.tf — gateway infrastructure (ALB, instances, security groups, Redis)","infra/terraform/network-isolation.tf — private subnet config for internal channel","webhooks/service/ — webhook subscription CRUD and delivery worker"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use gateway-native plugin architecture (Kong plugins, Envoy filters, or AWS API Gateway authorizers depending on platform choice). Keep plugins stateless — all state in Redis or config. Follow 12-factor principles for gateway instances. Use structured logging with correlation IDs (X-Request-ID) across gateway and all upstream services.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it handles identity and traffic management only","Do NOT transform request/response bodies in the gateway — this creates tight coupling and performance overhead","Do NOT use the gateway for service-to-service (east-west) traffic — use a service mesh for that","Do NOT hardcode rate limits — make them configuration-driven from day one","Do NOT skip the shadow mode phase — going straight to enforcement will cause outages from routing bugs","Do NOT share rate limit counters across consumer types — a partner's traffic must never affect a mobile user's limits","Do NOT store API keys in plaintext — store hashed keys, compare on lookup","Do NOT rely solely on network isolation for internal security — add mTLS or service mesh identity as defense in depth"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":85,"high_confidence_areas":["Three-channel ingress separation is the right model for these consumer types","Centralized auth at gateway eliminates the inconsistent auth anti-pattern","Sliding window rate limiting with Redis is well-proven at this scale","Strangler fig migration is the only safe approach for a running system","Header enrichment as the gateway-to-service contract is standard and proven"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway technology choice (Kong vs Envoy vs cloud-native) depends on team expertise and existing infrastructure — not enough context to recommend","Webhook delivery SLA requirements not specified — retry policy may need adjustment","Whether internal services should use mTLS or service mesh depends on existing infrastructure maturity","Redis cluster sizing depends on actual traffic volume which isn't specified"],"assumptions":["The platform runs on cloud infrastructure with VPC/private subnet capabilities","Traffic volume is moderate enough that a single Redis cluster can handle rate limit checks (up to ~100K req/s)","The team has operational capacity to manage a gateway cluster (or will use a managed service)","Existing services can be modified to read enriched headers and eventually remove auth code","Partner count is in the hundreds, not tens of thousands (affects API key lookup strategy)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress channels (mobile, partner, internal), centralizing cross-cutting concerns while maintaining strict traffic isolation. This eliminates the current anti-pattern of distributed auth implementations and creates a single enforcement point for security, rate limiting, and observability.","ar
